Interaction 1.0: A Database of Protein-Protein Interactions

"Connect the dots"- A search tool for patterns of protein interaction

Data entry and modification tools for protein interactions database

The sheer quantity of genomic data has forced the development of computational methods to store and examine it. Sophisticated databases and search tools have been developed for manipulating sequence and expression data. Genomic data on protein- protein interactions can be mass-produced by the two-hybrid system, which was been pioneered by our laboratory and that of Stan Fields.

A key technique in mass-producing such data is 'interaction mating', in which potentially interacting proteins are expressed in bait and prey constructs in yeast strains of each mating type. Pairwise combinations of potentially interacting proteins can then be tested, simply by mating bait-containing and prey-containing strains together to construct diploids with a bait and a prey.

To deal with this data, we have created a database structure and tools for manipulating sets of protein interaction data. "Connect the dots" allows the user to input a graph representing a set of protein interactions. The data entry and modification tools allow a user to enter and modify interaction data, and to search through the data based on names or descriptions of proteins, rather than patterns of interaction.

With this release, we make available a database of protein protein interactions, as well as tools for searching the data. During the next year, we will:

  1. add new information to the core database
  2. make available a 'satellite' database to which other researchers can add their own data
  3. add to the core database a new type of interaction information, resulting from ongoing work based on an inventory of the interactions permitted among protein substructures (domains and subdomains; e.g. see SCOP). The ability to predict such structures from sequence is developing rapidly, and coupling this predictive ability with interaction data may allow prediction of protein interactions from sequence alone.

In future releases, we will also make available revised versions of the search tools and the data input and output tools. During the next year, as we write them, we will also make available programs to help integrate interaction data with other types of genomic data.

Proteins in the database

The database presented here contains 15,456 binary protein interaction assays among 591 proteins and revealing 452 interactions. These interactions have been identified through two-hybrid methods by experimenters in the Brent group. Many of the proteins have to do with cell-cycle control.

Methods. The data in the database derives from two experiments. In the first experiment, 21 preys (OMI1782, p16, p18, p21, p27, touvlo, cdk2, cdk4, cdk6, cdk6, cycD, CdcE, cks2, cks1, cdi1, Rb, Mxi, Yrb1, Yrb2, Nup2, ran, and Gsp1, Gsp2) were tested against 576 baits. The data in the database was generated by Alex Reymond in Roger Brent's lab between 1995 and 1997. Most of the data in this experiment was independently replicated by Andy Mendelson, Barak Cohen, and C. Wilson Xu.

In the second experiment, 7 preys (Prg1, Prg2, Cart1, Prg12, Prg14, Prg37, and Prg38) were tested against 480 baits, comprising a subset of the 576 baits from experiment 1. This experiment was performed by Pierre Colas. Both experiments were performed by transforming the baits and preys into EGY48(pSH18-34) and EGY42, respectively, and performing replica-matings as described by Finley et al. (1994).

Credits

The database, search programs, and data input and output programs were made by the computer science research fellows at TMSI. Larry Lok developed the pattern search algorithm and the user interface. Bob Cohen developed the data entry and modification tools. Roger Brent worked with Larry Lok to define the need to search through interaction data. Jeffrey Way helped test these tools and write explanations as to how to use them. The database is implemented in the Informix Universal Server, which was granted to us by Informix under their 'Engines of innovation' program. This work was supported by a grant from the National Institute for Human Genome Research to the Brent lab ("Defining gene function by protein-protein interaction").

References

  • Annu Rev Genet 1997;31:663-704 Understanding gene and allele function with two-hybrid methods. Brent R, Finley RL Jr
  • Cell 1993 Nov 19;75(4):791-803 Cdi1, a human G1 and S phase protein phosphatase that associates with Cdk2. Gyuris J, Golemis E, Chertkov H, Brent R
  • Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A 1994 Dec 20;91(26):12980-4 Interaction mating reveals binary and ternary connections between Drosophila cell cycle regulators. Finley RL Jr, Brent R
  • Trends Genet 1994 Aug;10(8):286-92 The two-hybrid system: an assay for protein-protein interactions. Fields S, Sternglanz R
  • Nature 1989 Jul 20;340(6230):245-6 A novel genetic system to detect protein-protein interactions. Fields S, Song O

Comments, questions and feedback may be directed to dbcomments@molsci.org
